content:
# Romans Chapter 4 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_004_page_001.jpg

1. What then shall we say that Abraham the forefather of us found according to [the] flesh?

2. For if Abraham was justified from works, he has a boast — but not before the God.

3. For what does the writing say? "And Abraham believed the God, and it was reckoned to him for righteousness."

4. And to the [one] working, the wage is not reckoned according to grace but according to debt.

5. But to the [one] not working but believing upon the [one] justifying the ungodly, the faith of him is reckoned for righteousness.

6. Just as also David speaks of the blessedness of the human to whom the God reckons righteousness apart from works:

7. "Blessed [are those] whose lawless deeds were forgiven and whose sins were covered;

8. blessed [is the] man to whom the Lord will not at all reckon sin."

9. [Is] this blessedness then upon the circumcision, or also upon the uncircumcision? For we say, "The faith was reckoned to Abraham for righteousness."

10. How then was it reckoned? Being in circumcision or in uncircumcision? Not in circumcision but in uncircumcision.

11. And he received [the] sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness of the faith, the [one] in the uncircumcision, so that he [would be] the father of all the [ones] believing through uncircumcision, for the righteousness to be reckoned to them,

12. and father of circumcision — to the [ones] not only from circumcision but also to the [ones] walking in the footsteps of the faith, the [one] in [the] uncircumcision of the father of us Abraham.

13. For the promise to Abraham or to the seed of him — [that] he [would be] heir of [the] world — [was] not through [the] law but through [the] righteousness of faith.

14. For if the [ones] from [the] law [are] heirs, the faith has been emptied and the promise has been nullified.

15. For the law produces wrath; but where there is no law, neither [is there] transgression.

16. Because of this [it is] from faith, that [it may be] according to grace, so that the promise may be firm to all the seed — not to the [one] from the law only, but also to the [one] from [the] faith of Abraham, who is father of all of us,

17. just as it has been written, "A father of many nations I have placed you" — before the God in whom he believed, the [one] making alive the dead and calling the [things] not being as being.

18. Who against hope, upon hope believed, so that he became [the] father of many nations, according to the [thing] having been spoken, "Thus shall the seed of you be."

19. And not having weakened in the faith, he considered the body of himself already having been deadened — being about a hundred years [old] — and the deadness of the womb of Sarah.

20. But toward the promise of the God he did not waver in unbelief, but was empowered by faith, having given glory to the God,

21. and having been fully convinced that what [God] has promised, he is also able to do.

22. Therefore also it was reckoned to him for righteousness.

23. But it was not written because of him alone that it was reckoned to him,

24. but also because of us, to whom it is about to be reckoned — to the [ones] believing upon the [one] having raised Yeshua the Lord of us from [the] dead,

25. who was handed over because of the trespasses of us, and was raised because of the justification of us.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.3 — Genesis 15:6 quotation:** ἐπίστευσεν δὲ Ἀβραὰμ τῷ θεῷ — "And Abraham believed God." This is the foundational text for Paul's entire argument. The verb πιστεύω (believe/trust) precedes any mention of circumcision (Genesis 17) or the law (Sinai). Chronology is theology: faith came first.

2. **v.5 — "Justifying the ungodly" (τὸν δικαιοῦντα τὸν ἀσεβῆ):** A shocking phrase. In the OT, justifying the wicked is condemned (Proverbs 17:15, Isaiah 5:23). Paul attributes to God what the law prohibits in human judges. This is the scandal of grace.

3. **v.19 — Sinaiticus reading:** Sinaiticus reads οὐ κατενόησεν ("he did not consider") without the negative in some early witnesses, creating the opposite meaning: "he considered" his aged body. The Sinaiticus reading emphasizes that Abraham's faith was not blind — he saw the impossibility and believed anyway.

4. **v.25 — Parallel structure:** παρεδόθη / ἠγέρθη — "handed over / raised." The two clauses mirror each other: his death was for our trespasses; his resurrection was for our justification. This is likely a pre-Pauline creedal formula.

— EMET, AI Translator
